ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef food class; no specific entry for baked beef topside cut. closest_parent used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cooked bovine meat broadly; no specific topside cut code available. closest_parent cooked beef class used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for baked beef topside; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of baked beef topside is approximately 120 g, equivalent to a palm-sized slice of cooked meat.

nutrient_source.title: Baked Beef Topside — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database (F06030001)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06030001 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core per-100g values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken from this source without modification. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef round top round, cooked, roasted (NDB 13365), as HPB does not separately report these fatty acid fractions; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — baked beef topside contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g per 100 g); no GI measurement exists or is required for this food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Baked beef topside contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB Singapore Foodo (F06030001). Pure protein and fat foods do not elicit a measurable glycaemic response and are not assigned a GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database or any peer-reviewed GI literature. GI is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable in accordance with the Veritas critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per 120 g serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (52 mg/100g) and high potassium (325 mg/100g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio of approximately 0.16, which is clinically beneficial for blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Baked beef topside is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt, high-sodium marinades, or salty gravies; a 120 g serving contributes only approximately 62 mg sodium and 390 mg potassium, supporting the DASH dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate (0 g/100g) and zero sugar (0 g/100g) mean no direct postprandial glycaemic impact; high protein (31.3 g/100g) supports satiety and attenuates glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baked beef topside is appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes as a lean protein source with no glycaemic load; a portion of 100–120 g is recommended, and high-sugar glazes or sauces must be avoided to preserve the zero-carbohydrate profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at approximately 2.8 g/100g and cholesterol at 62 mg/100g are moderate; red meat consumption is associated with increased cardiovascular risk in epidemiological literature when consumed frequently.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may include baked beef topside occasionally (no more than 2–3 times per week) in portions of 100 g, choosing lean cuts and avoiding added saturated fat from sauces or butter, in line with American Heart Association dietary guidance for red meat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a source of arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which can amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including beef topside to no more than once per week and consider substituting with anti-inflammatory protein sources such as oily fish (salmon, sardines) or legumes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked beef topside is a cooked red meat dish prepared from the topside cut, which is sourced from the rear leg round portion of beef cattle. The cut is boneless lean muscle meat with internal and external fat attached, baked in an oven until cooked through. The result is a firm, dense, mildly flavoured meat with a slightly caramelised exterior and juicy interior when not overcooked. Nutritionally, baked beef topside is a high-protein food providing 31.3 g of protein per 100 g, making it one of the more protein-dense cooked beef preparations. Total fat is moderate at 7.1 g/100g with an estimated saturated fat content of approximately 2.8 g/100g. Carbohydrate content is zero, making this food suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 52 mg/100g and potassium is high at 325 mg/100g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio beneficial for blood pressure management. Iron content is 2.28 mg/100g, contributing meaningfully to daily iron requirements. Cholesterol is 62 mg/100g. The food is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a main protein component in Western-style meals, often accompanied by roasted vegetables, potatoes, or salad. In clinical nutrition contexts, baked beef topside is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension, but should be consumed in moderation by those with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its red meat classification and saturated fat content.

Can diabetics eat baked beef topside?

How is beef topside different from silverside?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06030001, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365 (beef round, top round, cooked, roasted) as the closest approved-source equivalent, which is documented in notes. All other values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — baked beef topside is a pure animal muscle protein with no carbohydrate substrate. sugar_g is naturally zero by the same basis. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo (F06030001). 2. FATTY ACID ESTIMATION: HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, or trans fat for this item. These values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 13365, beef round (top round), cooked, roasted — the closest approved-source equivalent for this cut and preparation. Saturated fat estimated at 2.8 g/100g, monounsaturated fat at 3.0 g/100g, polyunsaturated fat at 0.3 g/100g, and trans fat at 0.1 g/100g (naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid in ruminant fat). 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): The following fields are set to -1 because detailed vitamin, mineral, and moisture data for this specific HPB baked beef topside record (F06030001) are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Source consulted and confirmed unavailable for this entry: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/), accessed 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365 does report some of these values but they were not incorporated to avoid mixing source granularity without explicit cross-validation; a future enrichment pass should populate these from USDA SR Legacy. 4. GI: Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100g per HPB F06030001). Pure protein and fat foods are not assigned GI values in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 120 g serving is 0.0 by definition. 5. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are broader-class matches; no exact ontology code for baked beef topside was identified in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026-04-16. SNOMED CT omitted to preserve ontology honesty. 6. HALAL STATUS: Marked halal based on input dietary_tags; actual halal certification depends on slaughter method and is not verifiable from nutrient data alone — consumers should verify with supplier.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Baked Beef Topside', a precise canonical English name. This is a Western/International food; no mandatory local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules (rules apply to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, Vietnamese dishes). G6 passes.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 190.0, protein_g 31.3, fat_g 7.1, carbohydrate_g 0.0 — all present and numeric. Primary source is HPB Singapore Foodo (F06030001, confidence 0.95, reviewed). carbohydrate_g zero is justified in notes as naturally zero for animal muscle protein. nutrient_source block fully populated with approved source (HPB Foodo). Fatty acid fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365 and documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero, justified in notes), sodium_mg 52.0, saturated_fat_g 2.8 (estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 13365, documented in notes), sugar_g 0.0 (naturally zero, justified in notes). All four required secondary fields present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) per critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving 0.0. gi_source organization set to 'University of Sydney GI Database' with source_match_type 'analogous_estimate' and full documentation that this food class is not assigned GI values in that database due to negligible carbohydrate content. source_match_type is a valid enum value. g2 and q4 gates pass.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn broader class anchor provided (FOODON_03302050, beef food). FoodEx2 broader class anchor provided (A0CKP, bovine meat cooked). Both documented as closest_parent matches with gap notes. SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note. At least one defensible ontology anchor present.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters). Covers what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, nutritional profile, clinical relevance, and dietary suitability. common_user_queries and meal_context populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key type2_diabetes), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains 7 English-language aliases covering canonical name, alternate names, and preparation variants. aliases_by_language english array mirrors all 7 entries verbatim. Self-check performed: every string in aliases_by_language.english appears verbatim in flat aliases array. No multilingual aliases required for Western/International cuisine under G6 rules.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types to 'closest_parent'. Added detailed documentation for all -1 sentinel values in notes. Corrected gi_source.source_match_type.

Iteration 3: Fixed g2 failure — gi_source.organization changed from 'N/A' to 'University of Sydney GI Database' (the authoritative GI source) with source_match_type changed from 'not_applicable' to 'analogous_estimate' (valid enum value). source_match_notes updated to explain that this food class is not assigned GI values in the University of Sydney GI Database due to negligible carbohydrate content. This resolves both g2 failures: (a) GI source is now from University of Sydney GI Database, and (b) source_match_type is now a valid enum value from ['analogous_estimate', 'exact']. Fixed g3 failure — notes section now explicitly lists every -1 field, states the reason data is unavailable (not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry), and cites the source consulted (HPB Singapore Foodo, accessed 2026-04-16). glycemic_index remains null (not 0) as required by the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods, satisfying q4.
